coredata.dc:title: The influence of near-infrared carbon dots on the conformational variation and enzymatic activity of glucose oxidase: A multi-spectroscopic and biochemical study with molecular docking

coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the exceptional biocatalytic properties of glucose oxidase (GOx) have spurred the development of various GOx-functionalized nanocatalysts for cancer diagnosis and treatment. Carbon dots, renowned for their excellent biocompatibility and distinctive fluorescence properties, effectively incorporate GOx. Given the paramount importance of GOx's enzymatic activity in therapeutic efficacy, this study conducts a thorough exploration of the molecular-level binding dynamics between GOx and near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs). Utilizing various spectrometric and molecular simulation techniques, we reveal that NIR-CDs form a ground-state complex with GOx primarily via hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces, interacting directly with amino acid residues in GOx's active site. This binding leads to conformational change and reduces thermal stability of GOx, slightly inhibiting its enzymatic activity and demonstrating a competitive inhibition effect. In vitro experiments demonstrate that NIR-CDs attenuate the GOx's capacity to produce H2O2 in HeLa cells, mitigating enzyme-induced cytotoxicity and cellular damage. This comprehensive elucidation of the intricate binding mechanisms between NIR-CDs and GOx provides critical insights for the design of NIR-CD-based nanotherapeutic platforms to augment cancer therapy. Such advancements lay the groundwork for innovative and efficacious cancer treatment strategies.

head.ce:title: The influence of near-infrared carbon dots on the conformational variation and enzymatic activity of glucose oxidase: A multi-spectroscopic and biochemical study with molecular docking

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the exceptional biocatalytic properties of glucose oxidase (GOx) have spurred the development of various GOx-functionalized nanocatalysts for cancer diagnosis and treatment. Carbon dots, renowned for their excellent biocompatibility and distinctive fluorescence properties, effectively incorporate GOx. Given the paramount importance of GOx's enzymatic activity in therapeutic efficacy, this study conducts a thorough exploration of the molecular-level binding dynamics between GOx and near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs). Utilizing various spectrometric and molecular simulation techniques, we reveal that NIR-CDs form a ground-state complex with GOx primarily via hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces, interacting directly with amino acid residues in GOx's active site. This binding leads to conformational change and reduces thermal stability of GOx, slightly inhibiting its enzymatic activity and demonstrating a competitive inhibition effect. In vitro experiments demonstrate that NIR-CDs attenuate the GOx's capacity to produce H2O2 in HeLa cells, mitigating enzyme-induced cytotoxicity and cellular damage. This comprehensive elucidation of the intricate binding mechanisms between NIR-CDs and GOx provides critical insights for the design of NIR-CD-based nanotherapeutic platforms to augment cancer therapy. Such advancements lay the groundwork for innovative and efficacious cancer treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a category of carbonaceous nanomaterials measuring <10 nm in size, exhibit unique optical properties [1]. Distinct from their blue or green fluorescent counterparts, near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) display pronounced near-infrared absorption, thereby reducing autofluorescence interference and photodamage in biological tissues while facilitating deeper tissue penetration [2]. These remarkable optical properties have motivated numerous researchers to explore the potential of NIR-CDs for effective medical diagnosis and therapy. Leveraging outstanding biocompatibility and notable NIR absorption, NIR-CDs are anticipated to be widely employed as excellent bioimaging agents, offering a solution to the toxicity associated with traditional organic dyes [2,3]. Moreover, NIR-CDs show promising applications in cancer diagnosis and treatment modalities, including photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, and tumor-targeted drug delivery [4–6]. However, the nano-scale dimensions of CDs can influence protein functionality by inducing conformational changes, thereby affecting their physiological role. Studies have indicated that CDs can penetrate protein interiors, altering their structure and function. For example, Liang et al. observed fluorescence quenching of human serum albumin by three distinct CDs derived from different carbon sources, and these CDs induced conformational changes in the protein through various quenching mechanisms [7]. Similarly, Wang et al. found that nitrogen-doping CDs delayed the formation of human insulin fibrils by forming hydrogen bonds, resulting in thinner and shorter fibrils [8]. Despite these advancements, research on the binding mechanism of NIR-CDs with enzymes remains limited. A comprehensive understanding of how NIR-CDs interact with and potentially affect enzyme catalytic activity is urgently needed. Such insights would be invaluable in realizing the full potential of these nanomaterials in biomedical applications.

Glucose oxidase (GOx), an oxidoreductase, specifically and efficiently catalyzes the oxidation of β-d-glucose, yielding gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with oxygen (O2) serving as the oxidant. Its significant potential has led to its increasing application in biomedical tumor multimodal synergistic therapy [9]. In recent years, various cancer therapeutic approaches have harnessed the biocatalytic properties of GOx. The depletion of intratumoral glucose and oxygen induces a nutrient deficiency and elevates hypoxia levels within the tumor microenvironment, facilitating cancer starvation therapy and hypoxia-activated therapy. Furthermore, the production of gluconic acid creates acidic conditions conducive to the release of pH-responsive drugs from a prodrug carrier, while H2O2 production enhances oxidative stress in tumors. Notably, through the Fenton or Fenton-like reaction, or under light irradiation, H2O2 can be converted into reactive oxygen species, such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which exhibit potent lethality toward tumor cells [10]. Explorations have been made into combination of carbon-based photodynamic therapy and photothermal therapy with GOx-induced starvation therapy. Zhang et al. have engineered a multifunctional poly(γ-glutamic acid)@glucose oxidase@carbon dot nanoparticles for application in starving therapy, photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, and immunotherapy [11]. This photosensitive nanoparticle facilitates the breakdown of the generated H2O2 into O2, which further transforms into reactive singlet oxygen (1O2) under 730 nm laser irradiation. During this process, the presence of GOx within the nanoparticle catalyzes the oxidation of intracellular glucose, producing in-situ H2O2. This significantly enhances the efficiency of photodynamic therapy and induces cancer cells to undergo starvation-induced death. The variation in generated H2O2 concentrations and pH values over time, when the nanoparticle is incubated with glucose, mirrors that of free GOx. This indicates that the GOx covalently bound to the nanoparticle retains its natural activity [11]. Moreover, Wibrianto et al. have designed a multifunctional nanocatalyst composed of copper, nitrogen, and sulfur-doped CDs, GOx, and camptothecin. When covalently linked with GOx, the CuCGC nanocatalyst demonstrates GOx-like activity. It breaks down intratumoral glucose into H2O2, amplifying the chemodynamic therapy effect by utilizing self-supporting H2O2. Experimental validation, using the standard TMB assay, has revealed that the catalytic oxidation activity of the CuCGC nanocatalyst is 18 % of the original GOx [12].

Multimodal synergistic cancer therapies utilizing GOx have indeed shown remarkable anti-tumor efficacy when compared to single therapeutic approaches. The effectiveness of the anticancer effect is intricately tied to the enzyme's catalytic activity. In the construction of CDs-based therapeutic nanoplatforms through the conjugation of GOx to CDs, it is vital to understand how nanoparticles impact enzymatic conformation and activity. However, there is limited research on the binding mechanism of GOx and CDs, particularly NIR-CDs. NIR-CDs, as a biocompatible photothermic agent with significant potential in biomedicine, could significantly enhance their efficacy in cancer therapy through biofunctionalization with GOx. Hence, there is a pressing need to explore the binding mechanisms of NIR-CDs and GOx. This research reveals that NIR-CDs form a ground-state complex with glucose oxidase, competitively inhibiting enzyme activity in a concentration-dependent manner. The inhibitory effect of NIR-CDs on enzyme activity has been assessed at both the biological level and from a biological perspective. These results provide theoretical guidance for the design of more effective GOx-based nanocomposites for cancer treatment.

N-(4-Aminophenyl)-acetamide (98 %) and 4,5-difuorobenzene-1,2-diamine (97 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. GOx derived from Aspergillus niger (>180 U∙mg−1), peroxidase from horseradish (>300 U∙mg−1), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (≥99.90 %), and 3,3′-dimethoxybenzidine (97 %) were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Inc. β-d-glucose (85 %) was purchased from Shanghai TCI Development Co., Ltd. Human cervical carcinoma cells (HeLa cells) and human gastric mucosal epithelial cells (Ges-1 cells) were both purchased from Shanghai Gening Biological Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified eagle medium was purchased from Thermo Fisher Instrument Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum was purchased from Shanghai XP Biotechnologies Co., Ltd. Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (≥98 %), trypsin-EDTA solution 0.25 % (with phenol red), and Penicillin-Streptomycin Liquid (100 ×) were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ∙cm was purified through a Millipore-Q ultrapure water purification system.

In accordance with the literature [13], NIR-CDs were synthesized by mixing N-(4-aminophenyl)-acetamide (0.15 g) and 4,5-difluorobenzene-1,2-diamine (0.047 g) in 20 mL of ultrapure water. After a 5-min ultrasonication, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-liner stainless steel autoclave and subjected to a hydrothermally reacted for 10 h at 180 °C. The resulting NIR-CDs were then purified by centrifugation at 11000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove any residual impurities. The filtrate underwent dialysis using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da for a duration of 24 h. After purification, the NIR-CDs were vacuum dried to obtain a solid powder and stored at 4 °C until further experiments.

Time-resolved fluorescence decay traces of both GOx and GOx − NIR-CDs system were acquired by the Horiba Scientific QM-8075 spectrometer at room temperature. The excitation was set at 278 nm, with an emission wavelength of 338 nm. The concentration of GOx was maintained at 6.67 × 10−7 mol∙L−1, and the concentrations of NIR-CDs were 0, 1.92 × 10−5 mol∙L−1, 3.84 × 10−5 mol∙L−1, and 7.68 × 10−5 mol∙L−1, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra of GOx, NIR-CDs, and the GOx − NIR-CDs system were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 UV–vis absorption spectrometer in the scanning range from 800 to 200 nm. The concentrations of GOx and NIR-CDs were 6.67 × 10−7 mol∙L−1 and 5.92 × 10−6 mol∙L−1, respectively.

Steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded using Thermo Scientific Lumina fluorescence spectrometer. GOx, at a fixed concentration of 6.67 × 10−7 mol∙L−1, was dissolved in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.5) and then titrated by NIR-CDs with increasing concentrations from 0 to 4.28 × 10−5 mol∙L−1 and the concentration interval was set as 4.28 × 10−6 mol∙L−1 in the meantime. Within the scanning emission spectral range of 305–450 nm, the intensities change of the maximum emission wavelength at 338 nm were analyzed under the excitation wavelength of 295 nm. Synchronous fluorescence spectra change of tryptophan (Trp) and tyrosine (Tyr) residues in GOx caused by NIR-CDs were determined under the same operating conditions with wavelength interval (∆λ = λ em − λ ex) set to 15 nm and 60 nm for Tyr and Trp residues, respectively.

Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence experiments were conducted on a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer. The excitation wavelength was in the range of 200 to 350 nm with a 5 nm interval, while the emission wavelength was increased from 200 to 600 nm. The scan speed was 600 nm∙min−1 with the excitation and the emission slit width of 10 nm and 8 nm, respectively. The concentrations of GOx and NIR-CDs were 6.67 × 10−7 mol∙L−1 and 3.84 × 10−5 mol∙L−1, respectively.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectral measurements were performed on Applied Photophysics circular dichroic spectrometer equipped with a thermostatic bath. All CD spectra were collected from 200 to 260 nm with a scan speed of 500 nm∙min−1 and a response time of 0.5 s at room temperature. Each spectrum was the average of three scans under the same conditions. The concentration of GOx was 5.0 × 10−7 mol∙L−1. The concentration of NIR-CDs was 2.14 × 10−5 mol∙L−1 and 4.28 × 10−5 mol∙L−1, respectively. The CD thermal spectra of GOx were collected from 20 to 94 °C with a 4 °C interval. The melting temperature (T m) and the molar enthalpy change (ΔH m) at T m were automatically calculated by Global Analysis Software, and circular dichroic neural network (CDNN) software was utilized to analyze the secondary structure information of GOx. The concentration of GOx was 6.67 × 10−7 mol∙L−1, while the concentration of NIR-CDs was 2.87 × 10−5 mol∙L−1 and 5.74 × 10−5 mol∙L−1, respectively.

The 3D-dimensional crystal structure of GOx, identified by the Protein Data Bank (PDB) ID: 1CF3, was procured from the PDB database [14]. Prior to ulitization, all solvent and organic molecules were meticulously eliminated from the structure. The molecular modeling of NIR-CDs was adeptly executed using the Chem 3D 19.0 software program. Subsequently, the Autodock 4.2.3 program was operated to simulate the binding interaction between GOx and NIR-CDs. The docking outcome that exhibited the lowest binding energy was methodically analyzed using Pymol software [15], providing insightful visualization of the binding orientation and interaction dynamics at the molecular level between GOx and NIR-CDs.

Drawing upon the methodology delineated in the aforementioned literature, the change in GOx enzymatic activity in the presence and absence of various concentrations of NIR-CDs was measured by the coupled enzyme method [16,17]. Briefly, a mixture was prepared by combining 0.1 mL of β-d-glucose (200 mg∙mL−1), 0.01 mL of HRP (0.08 mg∙mL−1), and 0.01 mL of 3,3′-dimethoxybenzidine (25 mM, dissolved in ethanol). This amalgamation was subsequently volumetrically adjusted to 1 mL with PBS (pH 6.5). To initiate the enzymatic reaction, 0.01 mL of GOx (0.016 mg∙mL−1) solution was added to the above mixture to react at 25 °C for 3 min, and the enhanced absorbance value was recorded at a wavelength of 460 nm utilizing a UV-2600 UV–vis absorption spectrometer. It is pertinent to note that an enzymatic activity of 1 U∙mL−1 GOx is analogous to the oxidation of 1 × 10−3 mol∙L−1 of H2O2 per minute under the experimental conditions. To elucidate the underlying inhibitory mechanism, the Michaelis−Menten model was judiciously applied to compute the Michaelis constant (K m) and the maximum velocity (V max) of GOx.

HeLa cells and Ges-1 cells were suspended in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 2 % penicillin-streptomycin, then plated in 96-well plates, and incubated in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. After a 24 h incubation period, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing varying concentrations of NIR-CDs. The cells were continually cultured for 24 or 48 h. Subsequently, the old medium was swapped with 200 μL of MTT solution (0.5 mg∙mL−1) and incubated for an additional 4 h. The resulting blue formazan was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and shaken slowly for 10 min. Cell viability was determined by measuring the optical density (OD) at 490 nm.

For the confocal fluorescence imaging of NIR-CDs in HeLa cells, HeLa cells were initially cultivated in a 35 mm glass bottom dish for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were exposed to fresh medium incorporating GOx, NIR-CDs, and GOx − NIR-CDs, respectively, and the incubation was continued for an additional 4 h. Finally, a confocal lase scanning microscope was employed using 488 nm laser light to capture the intricate cellular morphology.

All experimental data were measured in triplicate and results were presented as mean ± SD. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA with GraphPad Prism 10.2.2 software, while data graphs were created with Origin 9 software.

NIR-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method by using N-(4-aminophenyl)-acetamide and 4,5-difluorobenzene-1,2-diamine as precursors. Fig. S1A displays the excitation-independent emission fluorescence of NIR-CDs across varying excitation wavelength ranging from 465 nm to 575 nm, which is indicative of their uniform morphology, as referenced in [13]. The fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs reaches a maximum at 675 nm when excited at wavelength of 525 nm. Additionally, as shown separately in Fig. S1B, NIR-CDs exhibit distinct absorbance peaks at approximately 254 nm and 520 nm, which may originate from the π → π* transition of the CC bond and the n → π* transition of both the CO and/or C–O/C–N bonds, respectively [2,13]. Furthermore, the average relative molecular weight of NIR-CDs, as determined through matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF-MS), is approximately 521.1 g∙mol−1, as illustrated in Fig. S2. Therefore, the possible molecular structure of NIR-CDs is illustrated in Fig. S3.

The biocompatibility of NIR-CDs was meticulously evaluated using MTT experiments, revealing a favorable cellular response in both HeLa cells and Ges-1 cells across a concentration range of 0 to 100 μg∙mL−1. As shown in Fig. 1A, the cellular viability remained consistently above 80 % even after a 48 h-incubation period, furthermore corroborating the non-toxic properties of NIR-CDs. To underscore the remarkable fluorescence imaging capabilities of NIR-CDs, confocal laser scanning microscopy was employed. The images captured of HeLa cells incubated with NIR-CDs displayed bright red fluorescence, emphasizing their remarkable potential for fluorescence imaging (Fig. 1B). This comprehensive assessment reaffirms the promise of NIR-CDs as a biocompatible and efficacious tool for a myriad of biomedical applications, particularly in the realm of cancer imaging and therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction patterns of GOx with NIR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The time-resolved fluorescence assay serves as an efficacious method for shedding light on the intricacies of the fluorescence quenching phenomenon, thereby facilitating the elucidation of the predominant fluorescence quenching mechanism, which may entail static, dynamic, or a synergistic interplay of both. In the case of static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of a protein remains unperturbed by external agents, attributable to the formation of a ground-state complex. Conversely, dynamic quenching is typified by a diminution in the fluorescence lifetime (τ) consequent to the introduction of external agents [18]. The influence of diverse NIR-CD concentrations on the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the GOx − NIR-CDs system is clearly presented in Fig. 2A. Notably, as the concentrations of NIR-CDs increase, the decay curves of the GOx − NIR-CDs system remarkably align with those of GOx in isolation. The fitted results, as depicted in Fig. 2B, highlight that the derived average fluorescence lifetime (<τ>) values for the GOx − NIR-CDs system manifest negligible perturbations, closely resembling the intrinsic GOx value of 2.05 ns. This consistent behavior strongly supports the conclusion that the fluorescence quenching process is predominately orchestrated by the static quenching mechanism [18].

The characteristic absorption peak at approximately 277 nm in GOx is primarily attributed to a specific class of chromophore, namely the phenyl rings of amino acid residues such as Trp, Tyr, and phenylalanine (Phe) [19]. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, various UV–vis absorption spectra were precisely measured. Upon careful comparison of the differential absorbance spectrum of the GOx − NIR-CDs system with the spectra of NIR-CDs alone (curve d in Fig. 2C) and GOx alone (curve b in Fig. 2C), a distinct non-overlapping pattern is evident. This noteworthy observation provides strong evidence for static quenching mode and unambiguously confirms the formation of a novel ground-state GOx − NIR-CDs complex between NIR-CDs and GOx during their binding reaction [20].

The intrinsic fluorescence emission peak of GOx, prominently observed at 338 nm upon excitation at 295 nm, is attributed to the fluorophore derived from the phenyl rings of Trp residues [21]. Consequently, the widely employed technique of steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, which investigates the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching under varying temperatures, can assist in detecting alterations in the microenvironment surrounding the Trp residue and offer more comprehensive insights into the interaction between GOx and other micromolecules. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the maximum fluorescence signal of GOx decreases steadily, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency attains a remarkable 89.23 % upon the sequential addition of NIR-CDs. This fluorescence quenching effect is dependent on the ratio of NIR-CDs, with the quenching magnitude escalating as the concentration of NIR-CDs increases from 0 to 2.49 × 10−4 mol∙L−1. This observation further suggests that the binding site of NIR-CDs is situated at or in close proximity to the Trp residue [22].

The fluorescence quenching process is elucidated using the classical Stern-Volmer Eq. [20]: I 0/I = 1 + K sv[Q]. Herein, I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities of GOx in the absence and presence of NIR-CDs, respectively. The [Q] signifies the molar concentration of NIR-CDs. K sv, which denotes the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and is calculated from the slope through the above equation, is commonly used to distinguish static or dynamic quenching mechanisms based on its behavior at different temperatures. Fig. S4 displays the fluorescence spectra of GOx after the addition of NIR-CDs with increasing concentrations at 298 K, 304 K, and 310 K. Fig. 3A shows the Stern-Volmer plots of the GOx − NIR-CDs system at 298 K, 304 K, and 310 K. The calculated K sv values are listed in Table S1. It is obvious that the calculated K sv values decrease from 1.66 × 104 L∙mol−1 to 1.55 × 104 L∙mol−1 and 1.49 × 104 L∙mol−1 as the temperature increases from 298 K to 304 K and 310 K, indicating a trend opposite to the rising temperatures. This suggests that the fluorescence quenching process is likely dominated by a static quenching mode rather than a dynamic one, indicating the formation of a novel ground-state complex between NIR-CDs and GOx [20].

To quantitatively characterize the binding reaction process, the intrinsic binding constant (K a) can be determined through steady-state fluorescence spectra analysis. Typically, the modified Stern-Volmer equation is employed to analyze data in a static quenching process [23]. This equation accounts for the formation of a ground-state complex and allows for a more accurate determination of the binding affinity between NIR-CDs and GOx. By fitting the experimental data to this equation, the K a value can be obtained, providing a quantitative measure of the strength of the interaction between the two molecules.

Indeed, I 0 and I symbolize the fluorescence intensities of GOx in the absence and presence of NIR-CDs, respectively. [Q] represents the molar concentration of NIR-CDs, and f a represents the mole fraction of solvent-accessible fluorophore. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the modified Stern-Volmer plot shows a strong linear relationship. The K a values, listed in Table 1 , are on the order of 104 L∙mol−1, indicating a moderate interaction between NIR-CDs and GOx [24]. In addition, the binding constant K a value decreases from 1.35 × 104 L∙mol−1 to 1.12 × 104 L∙mol−1 and 0.99 × 104 L∙mol−1 when the reaction temperature increases from 298 K to 304 K and 310 K. This trend further corroborates the formation of a novel ground-state complex between GOx and NIR-CDs. The decrease in K a with increasing temperature is consistent with the behavior expected for a static quenching process, reinforcing the evidence for the complex formation [20].

In an alternative approach, quantitative information about the values of the equilibrium binding constant (K b) and the number of binding sites (n) for the interaction of GOx and NIR-CDs can be obtained by analyzing the fluorescence quenching data using the following double-logarithmic Eq. [23]:

The corresponding values of K b and n are indeed derived from the intercept and slope of the linear plot of log[(I 0 − I)/I] versus log[Q], respectively. Fig. 3C shows the double logarithmic fitting plots of GOx − NIR-CDs system at 298 K, 304 K, and 310 K. As presented in Table S2, the obtained values of n are close to 1 at diverse temperatures, probably revealing that, on average, only one NIR-CDs particle interacts with GOx at a time [25]. This finding implies a specific binding mode where a single NIR-CDs particle reacts with GOx, possibly occupying a single binding site on the enzyme. Results also shows the variation of K b (from 1.60 × 104 L∙mol−1 to 1.46 × 104 L∙mol−1 and 1.36 × 104 L∙mol−1) along with rising temperature, reinforcing that the GOx − NIR-CDs complex becomes less stable at higher temperatures. This is consistent with the general thermodynamic principle that increasing temperature can weaken non-covalent interactions, leading to a reduction in binding affinity. Moreover, the K b value for the GOx − NIR-CDs system is on the order of 104 L∙mol−1 [24,25], indicating a moderate binding affinity between GOx and NIR-CDs. This moderate affinity suggests that the interaction is neither too weak nor too strong, allowing for effective binding while also facilitating potential dissociation when necessary. Overall, these results provide valuable insights into the nature and stability of the GOx − NIR-CDs complex under different temperature conditions.

Typically, the binding process between GOx and NIR-CDs is facilitated by non-covalent forces such as van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interactions, and electrostatic interactions. The precise elucidation of these primary non-covalent driving forces can be achieved through thermodynamic parameters, including the free energy change (ΔG), the enthalpy change (ΔH), and the entropy change (ΔS). The magnitudes of these parameters can be calculated using the van't Hoff Eqs. [26]:

ΔG = ΔH − TΔS
Here, R is the gas constant (8.314 J∙mol−1∙K−1). Fig. 3D shows the Van't Hoff plot of GOx − NIR-CDs system at 298 K, 304 K, and 310 K. The calculated results presented in Table 1 clearly show that both ΔH (−38.89 kJ∙mol−1) and ΔS (−50.58 J∙mol−1∙K−1) are negative at the corresponding temperature, indicating the predominance of van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding. The negative values of ΔG at all temperatures signify that the binding of NIR-CDs to GOx occurs spontaneously [26]. This spontaneity arises from the favorable energetics of the interaction, even though it is accompanied by a decrease in entropy. The negative ΔG suggests that the energetic stabilization provided by van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding outweighs the entropic penalty associated with the binding event. Furthermore, the presence of common metal ions (such as Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+) was found to inhibit the binding of NIR-CDs to GOx [26]. This inhibition is reflected in the reduced binding constant compared to the GOx − NIR-CDs system in the absence of these metal ions (as shown in Table S3). The inhibitory effect of metal ions could be due to their competition with NIR-CDs for binding sites on GOx or through electrostatic interactions that disrupt the formation of the GOx − NIR-CDs complex. In summary, the thermodynamic analysis reveals that the binding of NIR-CDs to GOx is driven primarily by van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding, resulting in a spontaneous interaction. However, this binding can be inhibited by the presence of certain metal ions, which may interfere with the formation of the complex. These findings provide valuable insights into the molecular mechanisms governing the interaction between GOx and NIR-CDs, offering a foundation for further exploration and application in biosensing and nanomedicine.

The molecular docking results, as depicted in Fig. 4 , reveal that 29 amino acid residues are implicated in the binding interaction between GOx and NIR-CDs. These residues, including leucine-29 (Leu-29), Tyr-68, phenylalanine-72 (Phe-72), isoleucine-94 (Ile-94), arginine-95 (Arg-95), serine-96 (Ser-96), glycine-97 (Gly-97), Ser-103, valine-106 (Val-106), asparagine-107 (Asn-107), Gly-108, Gly-109, threonine-110 (Thr-110), Trp-111, Thr-112, Gly-290, Ser-291, alanine-292 (Ala-292), Arg-512, Asn-514, Tyr-515, histidine-516 (His-516), Gly-517, Gly-549, His-559, Val-560, methionine-561 (Met-561), Thr-562, and Phe-564, are pivotal to the binding process. The estimated free energy change (ΔG) is calculated to be −7.12 kcal∙mol−1, indicating a robust binding affinity between GOx and NIR-CDs. The final intermolecular energy (E inter-mol) of −9.8 kcal∙mol−1 further attests to the stability of this complex. The cumulative energy from van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, and desolvation energies (E VHD) amounts to −10.51 kcal∙mol−1, while the electrostatic energy (E elec) contributes +0.71 kcal∙mol−1. The final total internal energy (E total) stands at −1.2 kcal∙mol−1, and the torsional free energy (E torsional) is +2.68 kcal∙mol−1. These energy values suggest that van der Waals forces are the dominant factor in the binding interaction [27]. Notably, among the active-site residues of GOx, five amino acid residues (Tyr-68, Arg-512, Asn-514, His-516, and His-559) were found to directly interact with NIR-CDs at specific distances: 1.8 Å, 3.7 Å, 4.5 Å, 2.8 Å, and 2.3 Å, respectively. This close proximity indicates that NIR-CDs bind in the vicinity of the active site of GOx [28], which is crucial for its enzymatic activity. The binding of NIR-CDs near the active site suggests a potential modulation of GOx's catalytic function, which could have important implications for the development of novel biosensors or therapeutic agents based on this enzyme.

The influence of NIR-CDs on the conformational structure of the enzyme GOx can be effectively investigated using 3D fluorescence spectroscopy. This technique provides a visual representation of protein's conformational changes through alterations in fluorescence intensities and peak positions. In the spectra of GOx alone (Fig. 5A and C), specific peaks labeled as ‘a’ and ‘b’ correspond to the Rayleigh scattering peak (λ ex = λ em) and the second-order scattering peak (λ ex = 2λ em), respectively [29]. These peaks are characteristic features of fluorescence spectroscopy, providing valuable structural information about the protein. Additionally, two distinct hump-like peaks, designated as Peak 1 and Peak 2, are evident in the spectra. Peak 1 is associated with the π → π* electronic transition of both Tyr and Trp amino acid residues, while Peak 2 pertains to the π → π* transition of the polypeptide backbone. These peaks offer valuable insights into changes in the tertiary and secondary structures of GOx [29,30]. Upon complexation with NIR-CDs, notable alterations are observed in the 3D fluorescence spectra of GOx − NIR-CDs system (Fig. 5B and D). Specifically, there is a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensities of both Peak 1 and Peak 2. For Peak 1, the intensity decreases from 876.4 to 388.3, and for Peak 2, it diminishes from 223.7 to 91.8. Furthermore, Peak 2 exhibit a pronounced red shift of approximately 6 nm. These observations suggest that the presence of NIR-CDs induces conformational changes in the GOx polypeptide structure [31]. The decrease in fluorescence intensities and the red shift of Peak 2 indicate alterations in the protein's tertiary and secondary structures, respectively. Such conformational changes could potentially influence the enzymatic activity and function of GOx, highlighting the significance of NIR-CDs' interaction with the enzyme.

To deepen our understanding of the local microenvironmental changes near the Tyr and Trp residues of GOx induced by NIR-CDs interaction, synchronous fluorescence spectra were conducted. This method enables sensitive focusing on these alterations. Essentially, the crucial parameter Δλ is consistently set as 15 nm and 60 nm for Tyr and Trp residues, respectively. By adopting this approach, we could detect the characteristic synchronous fluorescence spectra of amino acid residues affected by NIR-CDs, thereby uncovering the structural modifications in GOx [32]. As depicted in Fig. 6 , with the gradual addition of NIR-CDs, the intensities of the maximal intrinsic fluorescence emission spectra for both Tyr and Trp residues decreased significantly. Additionally, a slight red-shift occurred, shifting from 302.6 nm to 304.4 nm for Tyr and from 342.4 nm to 344.4 nm for Trp. These shifts indicate an increased polarity and decreased hydrophobicity in the immediate environment of the amino acid residues. In essence, the conformational disruption of GOx caused by NIR-CDs resulted in greater exposure of both residues to the solvent, leading to a perturbed polar environment and a more hydrophilic condition around the chromogenic groups (Tyr and Trp) [33].

The interaction of micromolecules with biomacromolecules can induce significant conformational changes, potentially impacting the physiological functions of the enzyme. To quantitatively assess these conformational alterations in the secondary structure when a biofunctional enzyme binds to a micromolecule, CD spectroscopy is an essential tool. As shown in Fig. 7A, native GOx present characteristic CD spectra of typical α-helix, with two negative bands near 208 and 218 nm in the UV region [34], while NIR-CDs do not show any absorbance signal within the wavelength range from 200 to 260 nm. Upon the increased concentration of NIR-CDs, a slight decrease in the intensities of these two helical bands is observed, suggesting minor conformational perturbations of GOx [35]. Using the CDNN software, we calculated the relative components of four common secondary structures in GOx [36]. As illustrated in Table S4, native GOx contains 26.0 % α-helix, 17.5 % β-sheet, 18.5 % β-turn, and 38.0 % random coil. Upon interaction with NIR-CDs at molar ratios of 43:1 and 86:1 to GOx, a noticeable decrease in α-helix and β-sheet content, accompanied by an increase in random coil content, was observed. The percentage of β-turn remains almost unaltered (Fig. 7B). These structural modifications provide evidence of induced perturbations in the secondary structure, indicating slight unfolding and relaxation of the polypeptide chain backbone of native GOx during its interaction with NIR-CDs.

As illustrated in Fig. 8 , it is evident that the percentages of α-helix and β-sheet structures in GOx decrease as the temperature increases from 20 to 86 °C, regardless of whether NIR-CDs are present or not. Concurrently, the content of random coil structures increases, while the β-turn content remains relatively unchanged. This trend suggests that elevated temperatures induce conformational changes in the native GOx, leading to a partial unfolding and a more disordered structure. The thermal denaturation parameters, T m and ΔH m, shed light on the thermal stability of proteins. Using Global Analysis Software, the T m and ΔH m values for native GOx were determined to be approximately (62.8 ± 4.8) °C and (184.6 ± 16.0) kJ∙mol−1, respectively. However, in the presence of NIR-CDs, these values decrease. Specifically, at molar ratios of NIR-CDs to GOx of 43:1 and 86:1, the T m values decrease to (56.9 ± 8.3) °C and (55.2 ± 6.3) °C, respectively, while the ΔH m values reduce to (135.1 ± 17.3) kJ∙mol−1 and (126.4 ± 21.9) kJ∙mol−1, respectively. The decline in both T m and ΔH m values suggests that the binding of NIR-CDs to GOx reduces the thermal stability of the protein [20]. This decreased stability may result from the reduction of α-helix content induced by the interaction between GOx and NIR-CDs, as observed in the CD spectra. Such conformational change, induced by NIR-CDs, compromises the enzyme's structural integrity, making it more vulnerable to denaturation. These findings highlight the impact of nanomaterials on the protein's structure and function.

The conformational changes induced by NIR-CDs in enzyme molecules can significantly impact the distinct properties of proteins. To assess this, we scientifically measured the oxidation of β-d-glucose catalyzed by GOx using a spectrophotometric activity assay. Initially, exposure to 2.5 × 10−7 mol∙L−1 NIR-CDs had only a minimal effect on GOx activity (Fig. S5). However, as the molar concentration of NIR-CDs increased, a dose-dependent inhibition of GOx activity was observed, reaching a maximum inhibition efficiency of 79.56 % compared to free GOx at the highest NIR-CDs concentration [37]. The conformational structure of proteins is pivotal for their function and plays a determinant role in the biological activity of enzymes. Proteins' functional expression relies heavily on their unique natural structure. Disruption of the secondary and tertiary structures of GOx can lead to a decline in its catalytic activity. Interestingly, despite this interaction, GOx maintains a considerable amount of its biological activity even after being exposed to NIR-CDs. This retention of activity can be attributed to the type of inhibition exerted by NIR-CDs (Fig. 9 ). The calculated K m value for free GOx is (14.71 ± 0.67) × 10−3 mol∙L−1 (Table 2 ), which is in close agreement with the values reported in previous studies [14,16]. After adding NIR-CDs, the K m value increases to (17.71 ± 0.51) × 10−3 mol∙L−1, and the V max value slightly decreases from (34.47 ± 1.18) × 10−6 mol∙L−1∙min−1 to (33.07 ± 1.43) × 10−6 mol∙L−1∙min−1. This suggest that NIR-CDs shows a competitive inhibition on GOx activity [38]. Molecular docking results reveal that NIR-CDs bind in close proximity to the site directly involved in enzyme catalysis, indicating that NIR-CDs and β-d-glucose compete for binding to GOx. At sufficiently high concentrations of NIR-CDs, GOx demonstrates a stronger affinity for NIR-CDs. When NIR-CDs bind near the active site of GOx, they reduce the enzyme's affinity for the substrate, preventing β-d-glucose from binding and thus causing a loss of catalytic activity. This finding concurs with the results obtained through molecular docking. The decrease in the k cat (V max/[GOx]) and k cat/Km values further supports the inhibitory effect of NIR-CDs on GOx activity [26,36]. Additionally, the reduction in α-helix content implies a loosening of the GOx structure, destabilizing its active sites. This makes it more likely for NIR-CDs to competitively occupy the active center, thereby hindering subsequent substrate binding processes and ultimately leading to a decrease in enzyme activity [17].

The Warburg effect is a hallmark of tumor cell metabolism, distinguishing it from normal tissue cells. Tumor cells, which are highly sensitive to glucose concentration shifts, mainly rely on the glycolysis for energy production. This metabolic adaptation necessitates a significant uptake of glucose to fuel their rapid proliferation. Consequently, tumor cells exhibit elevated glucose levels compared to normal cells, reflecting their profound reliance on this energy source [9]. GOx, a widely employed enzyme in cancer treatment, specializes in catalyzing the oxidation of β-d-glucose to generate gluconic acid and H2O2. Given the importance of glucose in cancer cell growth and metabolism, the introduction of GOx into the cellular environment depletes glucose, inducing a state of nutritional deprivation in cancer cells and impeding their proliferative capacity. Additionally, the production of H2O2 exacerbates cytotoxic effects, further suppressing cell proliferation [39]. The enriched glucose content in tumor cells is conducive to enhancing the generation of H2O2 catalyzed by GOx. So to evaluate the effect of NIR-CDs on the catalytic function of GOx in cancer therapy, the MTT assay was employed to indirectly evaluate alterations in enzymatic activity following the introduction of NIR-CDs. Such assay provides insights into the potential modulatory effects of NIR-CDs on the therapeutic efficacy of GOx.

The cytotoxicity assessment of GOx on HeLa cells in the presence of varying concentrations of NIR-CDs, as evaluated by the MTT assay, is shown in Fig. 10 . After treating HeLa cells with GOx (1.6 × 10−9 mol∙L−1) for 4 h, cell viability dropped sharply to 36.13 %. This significant decrease is attributed to the synergistic effects of starvation induced by glucose depletion and the toxicity of H2O2 generated by GOx's enzymatic activity. This observation is consistent with finding reported in other studies where cancer cells were treated with GOx [40]. The cytotoxicity of GOx against HeLa tumor cells has also been evaluated and confirmed by others research groups [41,42]. Zhang et al. used a 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate probe on HeLa cells incubated with GOx and observed bright green fluorescence under confocal laser scanning microscopy, which confirmed the production of H2O2 through GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose [42]. H2O2 can easily damage cell membranes and cause oxidative DNA damage, leading to a decrease in cell viability. Qiu et al. found that GOx is not effectively taken up by HeLa cells during incubation [43], suggesting that GOx likely catalyzes glucose in the medium to produce H2O2, thus cutting off the nutrition source for cancer cells. Our experimental results align with these previous findings. As exhibited in Fig. 10, when the concentration of added NIR-CDs was 0.42 μg∙mL−1, the cell survival rate of HeLa cells was restored to 45.70 % of the original. When the concentration of added NIR-CDs was 1.25 μg∙mL−1, the cell survival rate of HeLa cells was significantly restored to 64.77 % of the original. This gradual recovery in cell survival with increasing NIR-CDs concentration suggests a slowdown in glucose consumption and a reduction in H2O2 production. The generation of harmful H2O2 relies on GOx's catalytic activity, which appears to be noticeably affected by exposure to higher doses of NIR-CDs, resulting in less cellular damage. This finding reinforces the inhibitory impact of NIR-CDs on the catalytic activity of GOx. However, at a NIR-CDs concentration of 2.92 μg∙mL−1, cell viability only improved to 68.14 % of the original, representing an increase of just 3.37 % compared to the 1.25 μg mL−1 concentration. This suggests that the inhibitory effect of NIR-CDs has reached saturation, even with a 2.3-fold increase in NIR-CDs concentration. Since GOx retains partial natural catalytic activity after binding with high concentrations of NIR-CDs, and NIR-CDs exhibit almost non-cytotoxicity to HeLa cells in same concentration range (Fig. S6), this is beneficial for effective utilization of GOx-mediated catalytic reactions in tumor therapy even after binding to NIR-CDs. These findings indicate the potential of NIR-CDs as an effective nanocarrier for GOx in future biological applications [11].

The comparative microscopic images presented in Fig. 11 depict normal HeLa cells and those incubated with GOx (1.6 × 10−9 mol∙L−1) for 4 h, both in the presence and absence of NIR-CDs. As shown in Fig. 11A, untreated HeLa cells appear healthy with intact cell membranes, well-defined nuclei, and a spread-out morphology characteristic of viable tumor cells. In contrast, cells treated with GOx alone (Fig. 11C) exhibit noticeable signs of contraction, with compromised cell membranes, dissolved cytoplasm, fragmented and condensed nuclei, and an overall reduced cell size. These changes are consistent with the depletion of glucose by GOx, which not only starves the cells but also generates toxic H2O2 that damages cellular structures [44]. Remarkably, when a higher dose of NIR-CDs (2.92 μg∙mL−1) is introduced along with GOx (Fig. 11D), there appears to be a protective effect on HeLa cells, as evidenced by the reduction in the cytotoxic effects compared to GOx treatment alone. This observation suggests that NIR-CDs can interfere with the catalytic function of GOx, potentially competing for active sites or altering the local environment in a way that diminishes GOx's ability to catalyze glucose oxidation. However, it is crucial to note that NIR-CDs do not completely inhibit GOx activity; thus, some degree of cell damage persists, leading to only a partial restoration of the normal cellular morphology. This indicates that even when combined with NIR-CDs, GOx retains its capacity to induce cancer cell death, albeit at a reduced efficacy. Conversely, when HeLa cells are incubated with NIR-CDs alone at the same concentration (Fig. 11B), they show no apparent adverse effects, underscoring the exceptional cytocompatibility of NIR-CDs. This further supports the notion that NIR-CDs can modulate the biological activity of GOx during cancer cell treatment, potentially offering a means to fine-tune the therapeutic efficacy of GOx-based interventions.

In summary, the spontaneous formation of a GOx − NIR-CDs ground-state complex occurs primarily through hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces. This complexation is observed when NIR-CDs interact in close proximity to the substrate binding pocket of GOx, leading to a competitive inhibition of its enzymatic activity. Notably, this inhibition is significant only at higher doses of NIR-CDs, where they can outcompete glucose in the binding process. Importantly, GOx retains much of its activity and native structure even after exposure to NIR-CDs, which is beneficial for effective tumor eradication. This binding study offers valuable theoretical insights into the formation of nanocomposites through the co-functionalization of NIR-CDs with GOx. Such understanding facilitates the design of GOx-based nanocatalysts for synergistic combinatory cancer therapy, thereby maximizing the potential of NIR-CDs in near-infrared image-guided synergistic cancer therapy applications.
